---
title: "From Citizen Input to Elite Legitimation: The Logic of Will-Confirmation in Constitution-Making"
sort: 1
author:
  - name: Matthew Martin
    url: https://mattjamesmartin.github.io/
    orcid: 0000-0003-3460-3603
    affiliation: Department of Government, University of Texas at Austin
categories: 
  - Chile
  - Constitutional design
  - Cuba
  - Political elites
  - Public consultation 
pub-info:
  reference: >-
    <strong>Matthew Martin</strong>. Forthcoming. “From Citizen Input to Elite Legitimation: The Logic of Will-Confirmation in Constitution-Making.” <em>Comparative Politics</em> 59(1): 000–000. doi: <a href="https://doi.org/10.5129/001041526X17731675177333"><code>10.5129/001041526X17731675177333</code></a>
  links:
  - name: Final version
    url: https://doi.org/10.5129/001041526X17731675177333
    icon: fa-solid fa-arrow-up-right-from-square
  - name: PDF (English)
    url: from-citizen-input-to-elite-legitimation.pdf
    local: true
    icon: fa-solid fa-file-pdf
  - name: PDF (Español)
    url: de-la-opinion-ciudadana-a-la-legitimacion-de-las-elites.pdf
    local: true
    icon: fa-solid fa-file-pdf
---

## Abstract

Constitution-making is cast as a state-society dialogue, with public consultation sustaining the legal fiction that “the people” write the social contract. I introduce will-confirmation—the interpretive practice whereby elites utilize underdetermined public input to legitimate representative claims. It has two modes: constructive (synthesizing ambiguous input into alignment) and dismissive (excluding contrary input as unrepresentative). Across thirty-seven interviews from Chile’s Constitutional Convention and Cuba’s Drafting Commission, I show how will-confirmation operates amid crises of representation. In Chile, drafters claiming to embody the people produced parallel monologues, aggravating rather than resolving a lack of elite consensus. In Cuba, drafters and regime-loyal intermediaries filtered input into centralized coherence, obscuring rather than remedying the absence of ideological pluralism. Will-confirmation reduced consultation to self-legitimation rather than a check on interpretation.{{< fa blank >}}
